Tethered Love: When Failures in Adult Development Forge Inescapable Archaic Bonds

Bruce Herzog M.D., F.R.C.P(C)

Bruce Herzog

Toronto
Ontario
Canada

Bruce Herzog   Bruce Herzog, M.D., F.R.C.P(C), is a graduate in Psychology and Medicine from the University of Western Ontario, Canada. He completed specialty training in Psychiatry and the Child/Adolescent Psychiatry program at the University of Toronto, and Psychoanalysis with The Toronto Institute of Psychoanalysis. He is a faculty member at the Toronto Institute of Contemporary Psychoanalysis, and associate editor of the journal Psychoanalysis, Self and Context. Dr. Herzog is a psychoanalyst in private practice in Toronto, Canada.CONTACT Bruce Herzog, M.D., F.R.C.P(C) bruceherzogmd@yahoo.ca 44 Riverview Drive Toronto, Ontario M4N 3C7, Canada

Character development does not end in childhood. It is a lifelong endeavor in which the undertaking of parenthood is a developmental stage. Parenting presents various challenges that promote maturation of the adult, while securing autonomy of the child. These adult developmental tasks include some that enhance abilities: respecting difference, accepting defiance, enduring anger, managing separations, and establishing identification with the child. In addition there are tasks that involve loss: relinquishing fantasies, tolerating disappointment, releasing the archaic bond, surrendering the helm, and withstanding one’s own abandonment. When the adult is incapable of successfully transiting through these parenting challenges, the child’s early ties to the primary caregiver are at risk of being abnormally extended. Parental refusal to relinquish the archaic attachment scuttles their offspring’s journey toward independence, and is referred to as “tethered love.” The concept of tethered love posits a pathogenesis of exaggerated compliance (or defiance) in children that begins with adult developmental failures in their caregivers. Clinical examples are used to underscore the suffocating impact of tethered love and demonstrate how the therapist must put aside feelings of distaste to better understand the child’s continuing connection to a constraining, even crippling, but often intensely loving parent.

Discussion of David Levit’s “Somatic Experiencing”

Jody Messler Davies PHD

Jody Messler Davies

New York, New York, USA
jodymesslerdavies@gmail.com

Jody Messler Davies   Editor-In -Chief of Psychoanalytic Dialogues from 1998-2008. She is Associate Clinical Professor of Psychology in the NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, Founding Vice President of IARPP, Founding board member of The Stephen Mitchell Center for Relational Studies, and coauthor with MaryGail Frawley-O’dea of Treating the Adult Survivor of Childhood Sexual Abuse. She has authored more than fifty articles on trauma, dissociation, multiplicity of self states, erotic transference-countertransference, termination, psychoanalytic imagination and analytic love.

The following is a discussion of David Levitt’s paper “Somatic Experiencing: Enhancing Psychoanalytic Holding for Trauma and Catastrophic Dissociation Contending with the Flood and Fog.” The discussion focuses on three major points: (1) the emphasis on psychophysiological hyperarousal and containment techniques in the treatment of traumatized adults has been a significant part of the relational trauma literature for at least thirty years. This author prefers to regard the significant contributions of Somatic Experiencing Techniques as evolutionary rather than revolutionary, building on much of the literature that has come before. (2) The notion that the self is not present during episodes of severe dissociation is challenged. This discussion asserts that during such episodes the patient’s “selves” fragment and a complex process of desymbolization occurs. However, some aspects of self are present to “take in” the non -verbal aspects of what the therapist has to offer. And finally (3) the relationship between patient and therapist is both a container in the Bionian sense, but also a trigger for the reenacted transference-countertransference paradigms that mark the treatment of traumatized patients. Although Levitt’s paper emphasizes the need for containment and holding, we cannot overlook the ongoing and ever-shifting presence of these self-other configurations as they saturate our therapeutic process. Here the therapist is both the container that makes treatment possible, but also the magnet that draws out these significant re-enactments.

“Looking, Standing, Moving, Connecting, Thinking,” A Psychoanalytic Mantra Through the Lens of Dissociative Disorders: Commentary on David Levit’s “Somatic Experiencing”

Elizabeth F. Howell PHD

Elizabeth F. Howell

New York, New York, USA
efhowell1@gmail.com

Elizabeth F. Howell   Faculty of the NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis; faculty and supervisor, Manhattan Institute for Psychoanalysis’s Certificate Program in Trauma Studies; honorary member of the William Alanson White Psychoanalytic Society; past Co-Director and Faculty, International Society for the Study of Trauma and Dissociation (ISSTD) Professional Training Program on Dissociative Disorders: Diagnosis and Treatment of Dissociative Disorders. In addition to over 40 professional articles, her books include: Trauma and Dissociation Informed Psychotherapy: Relational Healing and the Therapeutic Connection, Norton, 2020; The Dissociative Mind; Understanding and Treating Dissociative Identity Disorder: A Relational Approach; The Dissociative Mind in Psychoanalysis: Understanding and Working with Trauma, (Howell & Itzkowitz), Psychoanalysts, Psychologists & Psychiatrists Discuss Psychopathy and Human Evil (Itzkowitz & Howell), and Women and Mental Health, (Howell & Bayes).

Responding to a deficit in the psychoanalytic literature on trauma treatment, David Levit contributes a way to interweave Somatic Experiencing (SE) with psychoanalysis in the treatment of highly traumatized and dissociative patients. I agree heartily with the need for interweaving and integration that Levit notes, but I add that other trauma/dissociation treatments, including treatment of dissociative identity disorder (DID), can be integrated as well. Especially for patients with dissociative disorders, dissociation-oriented treatments are appropriate. Finally, I describe how integrations of the type Levit proposes, including other trauma/dissociation modalities, if they are guided by psychoanalytic principles, can be properly considered psychoanalysis, and come under the rubric of “trauma-analysis.”

David Levit PHD ABPP SEP

David Levit

Cambridge, Massachusetts, USA
dblevit@crocker.com
401 Main Street, Suite 16, Amherst, MA 01002

David Levit   Diplomate in Psychoanalysis and in Clinical Psychology. He is a psychoanalyst and a Somatic Experiencing Practitioner. He is Faculty and Supervising Analyst at the Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis (MIP) and Co-Founder, Chair, and Faculty of the MIP Postgraduate Fellowship-West Program. He is Instructor in Psychiatry, University of Massachusetts Medical School, and former Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry at Tufts Medical School. He is in private practice in Amherst, Massachusetts.

I appreciate Elizabeth Howell’s creative ways of re-articulating and engaging with my work with Ellie, and I respond here to her elaboration of her own generative thinking in terms of dissociation and DID. In dialogue with Jody Davies, I reply to her critique in terms of my view that she overly privileges the capital E enactment model in relation to other aspects of psychoanalytic process. However, that debate is not what is most central for me here. Regardless of how Davies, or you, the reader, might regard my own particular ways of engaging in the treatment with Ellie, the raison d’etre for my current paper (as well as an earlier one also on Somatic Experiencing is much more wide ranging. While I have described the SE model and illustrated how I interweave it into my psychoanalytic work, I seek more broadly to convey how we may draw upon SE to enhance aspects of our psychoanalytic work, regardless of our theoretical allegiances or beliefs. I elaborate here on SE’s organic, bottom-up ways of working with the body, how these truly differ from modes utilized by most analysts, and how SE can enfold into psychoanalytic treatment in both exploratory and regulatory processes, and in the therapeutic relationship. Howell’s discussion and Davies’ discussion/critique were quite generative in pushing me to clarify and deepen my thinking and to elaborate further here on the essence of Somatic Experiencing and what it has to offer to psychoanalytic treatment.

In This Issue, Psychoanalytic Dialogues 32-4

Lauren Levine PHD , Jack Foehl PHD , Stephen Hartman PHD and Amy Schwartz-Cooney PHD

Amy Schwartz-Cooney

Amy Schwartz-Cooney  

We assemble this issue of Psychoanalytic Dialogues 32-4 alert to the backdrop of a horrific war raging in Ukraine that exacerbates an already stark global refugee crises and accentuates a human capacity for unchecked cruelty, violence, racism, xenophobia and nationalism. The Ukraine war unfolds in the context of the ongoing exhaustive impact of COVID-19, a full-scale attack on women’s reproductive rights expressed in the U.S. Supreme court draft decision on Roe vs. Wade, legislative curbs on the freedom to articulate one’s embodiment in gender and to educate children about sexuality, the continued collapse of Truth and, with it, the “social container,” and a climate emergency that is already ravaging our planet and promises a myriad of future environmental catastrophes.

There is an individual and collective sense of helplessness in the face of all this global trauma. People face each day with a heightened sense of risk and uncertain future reward. What is our role as psychoanalysts and as editors, individually and collectively, in this desperately challenging time? In the words of Jeremy Ornstein, whose call to arms is featured in our collection of Snapshots on the climate crisis and psychoanalysis: “How do we spread urgency without magnifying our own fear or cynicism? How do we raise the alarm bells without bursting our eardrums?”

Taken together, the Snapshots, papers, and discussions in this issue address hope and hopelessness. Each author locates their inquiry in an atmosphere of collective concern: How can we maintain hope and sustain connection when all seems lost; how do we remain present and alive in the company of Others whose spirit has been trampled by developmental and racial trauma? How can we accompany our patients, not abandon them in their dread and despair while finding ways of staying affectively alive ourselves? How does the analyst remain robust in therapeutic conversation while also parrying feelings of shame, experiences of exclusion, inevitable encounters with aging that delimit what one might achieve in a career, and political and material realities that not only seem to cut life short - they just well may?

Climate Crisis and Psychoanalytic Responsibility: “There Is No Such Thing…”

Marie Saba MA

Marie Saba

Lima, Peru

Marie Saba  

Is there a relationship between psychoanalysis and the environment? It was decades ago since the affirmative answer was evident. Nevertheless, we can think about why the psychoanalytic world has taken so long to react and why there is not enough work related to the climate crisis.

We can begin by imagining Freud in 1922, concentrating on the intrapsychic register, trying to work during the roaring twenties, while the deafening sound of the war that had passed and the one to come, of the nearby deaths, racism, destructive repetition, felt deadening. However, he had written “Beyond the pleasure principle,” where he proposed the death drive: an original sin that seems to give his theory the missing part. Our admired patriarch feels less vulnerable.

Fast forward. Klein's theory was interpreted by most Kleinians as a mandate to leave external reality out of the consulting room; any reference to what was not part of the patient’s inner world was “light,” psychoanalysis. It was a symptom when a patient talked about politics. If the therapist did so, he was not respecting the basic rule of the game: neutrality. Politics? Climate change? You don’t talk about such things in psychoanalysis; there are more profound things to deal with. External reality is ignored, denied or factored out.

Nevertheless, “there’s no such thing … ”

Winnicott helped the talking cure become a listening, seeing, and acting cure. He dared to leave the intrapsychic confines and discovered real spaces between realities: long tethered relational seeds found fertile ground. He marveled with what he found (and created?) between humans. Marveled and worried: he could hear the roar of bombers and could see the deafness of his colleagues.

More than one hundred years after Freud’s most influential articles, we deal with other confinements. We are reminded that divisions are fictitious. We are in the same boat; neither the human mind nor the world is separated into bits and pieces. The Relational turn has added culture, ethics, values, and commitments to the psychoanalytic purview: a psychoanalytic clinical practice focused only on transference and resistance limited by linear and dichotomous thinking is considered senseless. While we often work with the past, we now focus on the present and the future. As proposed by Atlas and Aron in Dramatic Dialogues, we now find ourselves in a practice where the prospective function, oriented to prevent, prepare, or anticipate what could happen, is integral to our work. An essential part of the psychic function is dedicated to elaborating, organizing, and deciding, turning fate into destiny. Borrowing Borges’ words, we work with “prophetic memory.

Medio-Passive Agency in Psychoanalysis: Responding to Hopelessness and Despair in the Therapeutic Relationship

Christian Sell PHD

Christian Sell

christian.sell@ipu-berlin.de
Stromstr. 3b, Berlin 10555, Germany
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-8460-3946

Christian Sell   Assistant professor of Theoretical Psychoanalysis at the International Psychoanalytic University Berlin. He is also a psychoanalyst in training (IPA) at the Alexander Mitscherlich Institute for Psychoanalysis in Kassel, Germany. His research interests include psychoanalytic theory and epistemology, mixed methods in psychotherapy research, psychosis research, and the therapeutic use of imagery.

This essay investigates the role of medio-passive agency in how psychoanalysts can respond to hopelessness and despair in the therapeutic relationship. We can reconstruct from Lear’s study on radical hope a mutually constitutive interplay between hope and creativity. The agency exercised in hope and creativity is medio-passive: the clinical agent incorporates an experience of powerlessness into her response to an ongoing internal process, she lets go. With Winnicott and Bion, this letting go can further be traced as grounded in faith - in an unconscious commitment to the continuous possibility of the good. When patients are incapable of standing in faith, the analyst can make her own faith accessible to them. This process is introduced here as relational medio-passivity and illustrated through clinical material.

Owning the Responsibility of Shame and Erasure. A Response to Shabad’s Article “Owing and Being Owed: Shame and the Responsibility Toward Other”

Cynthia Chalker M.S.S. LCSW

Cynthia Chalker

msschalknyc@gmail.com
275 Central Park West, Suite 1F, Office 2, New York, NY 10024, USA

Cynthia Chalker   Black queer clinical social worker and psychoanalyst who lives and works in New York City. Her research interests include the intersection of race, culture and identity in psychoanalysis. She is an Associate Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues, an instructor at the National Institute for the Psychotherapies, is published in several psychoanalytic journals and has presented at conferences in the United States and internationally.

In response to Shabad’s paper, Owing and Being Owed, I query how the analyst and patient are positioned with regard to their social milieu and historical rank in the scene of a greeting that goes unmet. The shared experience of shame in such a scene too often eludes us precisely because of the invocation of shame. In turning away from what is shared or disparate in the shame that is summoned when a mixed-race dyad encounters the as-if neutrality of the frame, that is, the whiteness of the profession, the frame is secured as if obvious. And, the patient’s demand for recognition is rendered as-if developmental rather than structural to society at large.

Problematizing interactions between longtime enemies, this discussion identifies an interpersonal hysteria (HS Sullivan, 1953) that obfuscates communication in anticipation of disapprobation and rejection. Julia Kristeva’s notion of the abject, (1982) a similarly pre-discursive effort to protect identity and esteem, at the same forters a reductive binary between the pure vs. the corrupt. D.W. Winnicott’s concept of potential space (1971) is offered as an alternative area of overlap, a creative space within which literal and symbolic “not quite conversations” develop between polarized nemeses, a movement toward dialogue and mutual recognition.

Dr Kabasakalian-McKay’s hopeful discussion, in which she evokes a meeting between traditional enemies, is countered by the author’s awareness of a perhaps phylogenetic loyalty toward one’s “people,” a response relatively inured to the reason and self-reflexivity required for reconciliation.

Francisco J. González MD

Francisco J. González

f.j.g@comcast.net
582 Market Street, Suite 305, San Francisco, CA 94104

Francisco J. González   Personal & Supervising Analyst, Community Psychoanalysis Supervising Analyst, and Co-Director of the Community Psychoanalysis Track at the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California (PINC). He is on the faculty of PINC and the NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychoanalysis.

This essay is a series of short meditations on Adam Blum’s “How to surf the oceanic feeling.” Working in identification with Blum, and through the heaping up of associations to theory and literature, it considers (among other things) the “unintended story” of the group implicit in Blum’s poetic paper.

The author follows the discussants directly to the ocean as they infuse the initial essay with the sensory richness and material placeness accessible only to the genuine participant-observer. The concept of timelessness is dimensionalized to incorporate the swell of history. The reply culminates in a brief amplification of two themes which might otherwise be drowned out by other waves: the sexuality of homo dyadics, and the muses that inspired these sea dreams.

[[RunningHead]]

On Our Own or Together - A Reply to Holmes and Straker

Stephen J. Anen PHD

Stephen J. Anen

USA
stephen.anen@gmail.com
1539 Jackson Avenue, Suite 230, New Orleans, LA 70130
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-9215-2877

Stephen J. Anen  

Both discussions from Drs. Holmes and Straker (this issue) resonate curiosity and introspection. This response attempts to meet them in collaborative synchrony to continue exploring the psychic impacts of privilege and how clinical work may meet such sociopolitical dynamics, both with clearer conceptualizations as well as a sense of caution regarding the role of psychoanalytic practice. Through their invitations to go further, together, I will try to consider the intersection between White privilege and White supremacy as well as further examine the conditions and capacities undergirding the constructive use of deserved shame. While opening up space for such change is primarily taken up at the level of the individual, the elaborative and reparative potential of shame lies in its togetherness, seeking to better bridge self with the ideal self as well as self with the other. Deserved shame holds prospect to reclaim the intersubjective nexus of shame as not just a space of misattunement, inferiority, envy, and aggression but for re-attunement and intentionality, wherein subjective and objectiveness awareness may transition and interpenetrate.

[[RunningHead]]

The Slap: Playing with Reality in Discussing Ed Tronick’s “Trauma Never Occurs Only Once”

Jack Foehl PHD

Jack Foehl

Cambridge, Massachusetts, USA
jackfoehl@gmail.com

Jack Foehl   Joint Editor-in-Chief of Psychoanalytic Dialogues. He is President of Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute where he is Supervising and Training Analyst and is Supervisor and Faculty Member of the Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis. He is a Clinical Associate Professor at NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis and Lecturer at Harvard Medical School. Jack is author of numerous articles on the theory, description, and work of psychoanalytic process emphasizing lived experience in the analytic setting. He is in private practice in Cambridge, MA.

Trauma is neither a one-time only event nor something that can be completely divorced from the ongoing flow of living. Tronick’s developmental perspective integrates trauma into the entire spectrum of engaging where the momentum of rupture and repair and the unending processes of meaning-making link the seemingly irreconcilable trauma of a slap with the childhood game of peek-a-boo. Psychoanalysis also understands trauma as repetitive and further, encounters it in the unique setup of the analytic session. From this perspective, trauma is neither simply a process that is objectively observed nor something reduced to an exchange between first-person subjects. The essence of the unconscious process of trauma in psychoanalysis is found in how the tear in the fabric of being is encountered as an intersubjective phenomenon, where change occurs through creative and reparative play that engenders more flexible and novel modes of relating emerging from an embodied and mooded ground of experience that is fundamentally shared.

responseSet.title: Who’s on My Couch? Considering BIPOC Subjectivity and the Climate Crisis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Who’s on My Couch? Considering BIPOC Subjectivity and the Climate Crisis

Chanda D. Griffin LCSW

Chanda D. Griffin

NY, USA

Chanda D. Griffin  

Are psychoanalytic theories expansive enough to apply to the BIPOC psyche with respect to the climate crisis, when both the BIPOC body and the earth serve as HOSTs for a parasitic white supremacist culture and capitalism (Moss, 2021; Wilderson, 2020)? When considering the historical context: Indigenous people slaughtered and displaced from their land, extraction of gold and fossil fuels, and slaves utilized for the extraction of land and for their black bodies, how do we interrogate this legacy and its current impact on BIPOC body/minds? Although we all live within a historical socio-political context of whiteness as a cultural construct and value system of competitive individualism and delusion of invulnerability (and dominion over animals, plants, and earth), this cultural history is also embedded in an economic system fueled by the joined exploitation of land and bodies of color thereby impacting the subjectivity of BIPOC differently.

A few months ago, I was asked to be a discussant for a panel on Climate Change. I was surprised as I had never spoken on the subject. I was informed that my perspective, as a Black analyst, would be valued surrounding the intersection of race and one’s relationship to the environment. Racial/ethnic location has historically been ignored or excised from the psychoanalytic discourse but given the current racial reckoning in the aftermath of George Floyd’s death, this was one of many discussions I had been asked to weigh in on. My decision to do so was not without conflict.

Although I am aware of the climate crisis-rising temperatures, poor air quality and the need to reduce carbon emissions, I asked myself, “Who’s on my couch?” Environmental anxiety was not at the forefront of my BIPOC patients’ concerns, certainly not in ways discussed by white colleagues. Or had I not been “hearing” my patients’ awareness and/or anxiety about climate change? How were my patients speaking to their climate anxiety out in the open but unrecognized by me? What was my relationship with the environment and the earth? What did I not want to know?

Anorexia Nervosa, Psychic Death, and the Subjugation of Need

Tom Wooldridge PsyD ABPP FIPA CEDS-S

Tom Wooldridge

San Francisco, California, USA
twooldridge@ggu.edu
2041 Bancroft Way, #310, Berkeley, CA 94704

Tom Wooldridge   Chair in the Department of Psychology at Golden Gate University as well as a psychoanalyst and board-certified, licensed psychologist. His books include Understanding Anorexia Nervosa in Males: An Integrative Approach, Psychoanalytic Treatment of Eating Disorders: When Words Fail and Bodies Speak (Relational Perspectives), and the forthcoming Eating Disorders (New Introductions to Contemporary Psychoanalysis). He is on the Editorial Board of the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, the Scientific Advisory Council of the National Eating Disorders Association, is Faculty at the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California and the Northern California Society for Psychoanalytic Psychology, an Assistant Clinical Professor at UCSF’s Medical School, and in private practice in Berkeley, CA.

The entropic body is a body-state that develops against a background of early trauma in which the environment has failed to regulate the child’s anxiety, leaving her unable to regulate her own anxiety or to turn to others for help in doing so. The term refers to Freud’s conception of the death drive as aiming toward a state of tension reduction. In this paper, I describe how the anorexic patient’s subjugation of need aims at the phenomenological sphere highlighted by the notion of the death drive, which is felt to offer a path toward relief from annihilation anxiety and a particular form of gratification. In anorexic patients, the dialectical relationship between the forces of life and death has broken down and the pull toward psychic death has become increasingly compelling.

[[RunningHead]]

Somatic Experiencing: Enhancing Psychoanalytic Holding for Trauma and Catastrophic Dissociation - Contending with the Flood and the Fog

David Levit PHD ABPP SEP

David Levit

Cambridge, Massachusetts, USA
dblevit@crocker.com
401 Main Street, Suite 16, Amherst, MA 01002

David Levit   Diplomate in Psychoanalysis and in Clinical Psychology. He is a psychoanalyst and a Somatic Experiencing Practitioner. He is Faculty and Supervising Analyst at the Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis (MIP) and Co-Founder, Chair, and Faculty of the MIP Postgraduate Fellowship-West Program. He is Instructor in Psychiatry, University of Massachusetts Medical School, and former Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry at Tufts Medical School. He is in private practice in Amherst, Massachusetts.

I continue here the project of interweaving principles and approaches from a somatically based trauma therapy, Somatic Experiencing (SE), into psychoanalytic treatment. I focus on patients with severe early traumas who are chronically vulnerable to states of catastrophic dissociative dissolution. When dissociation takes this form, not merely compartmentalization, but dis-mentalization, there is no self who is present, who can hear, take in, and participate in desperately needed holding and containment. Traumas in the patient’s history and mind/body, which are precariously held behind a dam of dissociation, can burst through with such violence as to annihilate any semblance of “going on being.” While this must occur in treatment, and while we must accompany our patients there, it is a terrible dilemma when the floods, and then the fog, interfere with reception of psychoanalytic provision. When this goes on for too long, it may become iatrogenic more than therapeutic. I review various forms of holding and containment, but focus on one specific type of holding, protecting the patient from annihilating impingements. While Winnicott emphasized impingements from the outer world, I extend this to impingements that burst through from within, the catastrophic states themselves. I present a case to demonstrate how my drawing upon SE substantially enhanced this form of holding, and how it interwove more broadly into my psychoanalytic ways of looking, listening, thinking, and responding. I also illustrate how a somatically based therapy, such as SE, can be interwoven in ways that are constitutive and facilitative of psychoanalytic modes of therapeutic action.

responseSet.title: Climate Justice and Radical Ethics
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Climate Justice and Radical Ethics

Donna M. Orange PHD Psy.D.

Donna M. Orange

Claremont, CA, USA

Donna M. Orange  

What is climate justice? Like every justice discourse, it means acknowledging and addressing injustice. It includes the psychoanalytic work of remembering (colonization, chattel slavery, and their lived continuance today) and working through. Working through includes the work of repentance, repair, and reparation.

Today we live in a war-ravaged world sustained in its injustice and violence by an international commitment to oil, gas, and coal. These are creating temperature rises at rates historically unknown, sea level rise, polar melting, desertification, rapid destruction of irreplaceable species, long and terrifying fire seasons, and forms of extreme weather we had never seen. Those who suffer most from the climate emergency are those who have contributed to it the least, our brothers and sisters from Latin America, Africa, and the middle East. Remembering what is happening, why it is happening, and the extreme injustice of what is happening is psychoanalytic work, whether we call it that or not. Remembering that at least half the greenhouse gases destroying our home planet have come during the past 25 years means we who are alive today have committed this creeping crime against humanity.

Yes, as with other historical crimes like settler colonialism and chattel slavery, much of the damage has become systemic, that is, so embedded in our lives that it forms a normative unconsciousness (thank you, Lynne Layton). Like the caste system Isabel Wilkerson describes in unforgettable detail, climate damage belongs to each of us and all of us. The formidable political and economic forces, known in ordinary language as greed and lust for power, seem insurmountable to the “each of us.” But as Jeremy Ornstein (this issue) calls out to “each of us” psychoanalysts, the young activists need our voices and our bodies. Even if we cannot join them on hunger strikes, we can teach our colleagues, our children and our grandchildren that climate emergency also means devastation for our hungry and homeless sisters and brothers around the world. We can walk or bicycle instead of driving or flying, as often as possible. We can stop buying “stuff” made by horribly underpaid workers and shipped to us at tremendous climate cost. I freely but sorrowfully admit that, having grown up with very little, I became a mindless consumer in my middle years, trying to compensate for my class shame, especially within the psychoanalytic community. Time for more psychoanalytic reflection and repentance.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

When Racialized Ghosts Refuse to Become Ancestors: Tasting Loewald’s “Blood of Recognition” in Racial Melancholia and Mixed-Race Identities

Dhwani Shah MD

Dhwani Shah

dhwanishah12@gmail.com
44 Nassau St Suite 370, Princeton, NJ 08542
USA

Dhwani Shah   Psychiatrist and psychoanalyst currently practicing in Princeton, NJ. He is a clinical associate faculty member in the Department of Psychiatry at the University of Pennsylvania School of Medicine and a faculty member at the Psychoanalytic Center of Philadelphia. He completed his residency in psychiatry at the University of Pennsylvania School of Medicine where he was chief resident and completed a fellowship in treatment resistant mood disorders at University of Pennsylvania School of Medicine. He has authored articles on topics ranging from neuroscience, mood disorders, and psychoanalysis. Dr Shah’s forthcoming book entitled The Analyst’s Torment: Unbearable Mental States in Countertransference - is due out in Spring 2022 published by Phoenix Publishing House.

Experiences of feeling haunted and of being in the presence of ghosts are prominent in narratives of patients/people of color in the United States and of mixed-race identity. A creative reading of Hans Loewald’s evocative statement on therapeutic action, the process of transforming “ghosts into ancestors,” is used to explore a way of being with and healing patients with mixed-race identities who are imprisoned in melancholic states. An extended case vignette of an Indian American psychoanalyst working with a patient with a mixed racial identity highlights racialized components of melancholia and illuminates specific countertransference states and enactments that can both impede and allow for the gradual and partial witnessing of racialized ghosts and their transformation into ancestors.

Clinicians have a debt to pay to our patients that includes speaking in language as experience near as possible to their subjective reality. In this discussion I endeavor to spell-out the internal transitional space in which dissociatively endowed ways of being interact with each other and find ways to interact with the world in the service of survival and toleration of overwhelming intense affects and memories of severe adversity. While agreeing with the big stuff written by Shalgi and Levy, the important small stuff that makes a big difference is highlighted as a way to support the efforts of forward-thinking clinicians like Shalgi and Levy.

[[RunningHead]]

Imagination and Fantasy: The Dialectic Nature of the Encounter with Trauma and Dissociation

Daniel Levy MD and Boaz Shalgi PHD

Boaz Shalgi

Tel Aviv
Israel

Boaz Shalgi   Boaz Shalgi, Ph.D., is a clinical psychologist, head of the doctoral program: “Psychoanalysis and its Interfaces,” teacher, and supervisor, the Psychodynamic Psychotherapy Program, Sackler Faculty of Medicine, Tel Aviv University. He is the Editor in Chief, “Sihot - Dialogues”: Israel Journal of Psychotherapy.CONTACT Boaz Shalgi, Ph.D. shalgiby@013.net.il 14 Zufit st. Hod Hasharon, Israel

This paper proposes a distinction between fantasy and imagination, and endeavors to investigate its interconnectedness with trauma, as well as its implications to the therapeutic encounter with the psychic areas of trauma, dissociation, and repetition. Whereas imagination is an active phenomenon which resides within the dialectic of inner and outer worlds, increases psychic movement and works in the service of linking self-states within the subject and with his or her fellow subjects, fantasy is a phenomenon which is caused by trauma and isolates the subject both from his core self as well as from any sense of reality and connection with others. The paper offers a detailed exploration of these two modes of being and experiencing and of the psychic structures which comprise them, and demonstrates the ways by which the movement between imagination and fantasy, each with its own unique qualities, can enable patient and analyst to encounter, together, the experiences which were traumatized and dissociated. The clinical implications are discussed, and a detailed vignette is presented in order to demonstrate the elusive ways in which fantasy acts between patient and therapist in an effort to resuscitate deadened parts of the patient’s traumatized psychic life and bring them to the world of imagination and human connection.

[[RunningHead]]

On the Intersubjective Creation of Time and the Importance of Enactment: A Response to Chefetz and Bartlett

Daniel Levy MD and Boaz Shalgi PHD

Boaz Shalgi

Tel Aviv
Israel

Boaz Shalgi   Boaz Shalgi, Ph.D., is a clinical psychologist, head of the doctoral program: “Psychoanalysis and its Interfaces,” teacher, and supervisor, the Psychodynamic Psychotherapy Program, Sackler Faculty of Medicine, Tel Aviv University. He is the Editor in Chief, “Sihot - Dialogues”: Israel Journal of Psychotherapy.CONTACT Boaz Shalgi, Ph.D. shalgiby@013.net 14 Zufit st. Hod Hasharon, Israel

In this discussion of Dr. Chefetz’s and Dr. Bartlett’s responses (this issue, 2022), we further explore the inevitability of enactment in the process of treating dissociation in the face of relational trauma. Trauma, we propose, attacks human subjectivity by rapturing its being-in-time. Instead of being the creator of time, the subject finds himself doomed to be the object of time and living in the perpetuation of repetition compulsion. Paradoxically, it is only through repetition that one can break this time-loop and begin to feel and change his or her traumatic past. In the act of repetition, one subjugates the other (and reality as a whole) to his own frozen dissociations, yet with that very act also invites the other into his frozen experiences. When the other “accepts” the invitation, i.e., “answers” with an invitation of his or her own, an intersubjective encounter of frozen repetitions – a “dissociative third” - is created, which enables one to reclaim his or her own lost experiences and re-create his sense of time.

[[RunningHead]]

Possibility Clouds Arising from a Close Reading of Civitarese and Berrini’s “On Using Bion’s Concepts of Point, Line, and Linking in the Analysis of a 6-Year-Old Child”

Caron Harrang LICSW, FIPA

Caron Harrang

Seattle
Washington
USA

Caron Harrang   Caron Harrang, LICSW, FIPA is a board-certified psychoanalyst and an IPA training and supervising psychoanalyst on faculty at Northwestern Psychoanalytic Society and Institute. She maintains a full-time private practice in Seattle, Washington (USA). Publications include “Painting poppies: On the relationship between concrete and metaphorical thinking” (2012); “Psychic skin and narcissistic rage: Reflections on Almodóvar’s The Skin I Live In” (2012); From Reverie to Interpretation: Transforming Thought into the Action of Psychoanalysis (D. Blue & C. Harrang (Eds.) (2016); and “River to rapids: Speaking to the body in terms the body can understand” In C. Harrang, D. Tillotson, & N.C. Winters (Eds.) Body as psychoanalytic object: Clinical applications from Winnicott to Bion and beyond (2021).CONTACT Caron Harrang, LICSW, FIPA caron@caronharrang.com 2510 6th Avenue, Unit 2208, Seattle, WA 98121

This discussion explores an ambitiously conceived paper by Giuseppe Civitarese and Chiara Berrini (this issue, 2022) as it attempts to shed light on Bion’s use of mathematical concepts—point and line—to better understand the process of symbolization and of desymbolization (e.g., attacks on linking) applied to the clinical situation. Employing the authors’ elaboration of Bion’s thesis that all psychical transformations occur and thinking develops (or fails to develop) within an analytic field marked by emotional experience, I show how this formulation is as relevant to reading a psychoanalytic text as it is to our work in the consulting room. Accordingly, my commentary focuses on how the authors’ theoretical argument and clinical vignette impacted me, which I liken to “possibility clouds” arising in the analytic field. I suggest that such an approach echoes the authors’ openness to “an extraordinary sequence of play” between analyst and patient that inspired their submission to this journal.

Owing and Being Owed: Shame and Responsibility Toward The Other

Peter Shabad PHD

Peter Shabad

pshabad@aol.com
7141 North Tripp Avenue, Lincolnwood, IL 60712, USA

Peter Shabad   Clinical Associate Professor of Psychology at Northwestern University Medical School. He is on the Teaching and Supervising Faculty of the Chicago Center for Psychoanalysis, and Teaching and Supervising Faculty of the Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis. He is also Supervising and Training Analyst at the Institute for Contemporary Psychoanalysis. Dr. Shabad is co-editor of The Problem of Loss and Mourning: Psychoanalytic Perspectives (IUP, 1989) and is the author of Despair and the Return of Hope: Echoes of Mourning in Psychotherapy (Aronson, 2001). Dr. Shabad is currently working on a new book entitled Seizing The Vital Moment: Passion, Shame, and Mourning to be published by Routledge.

In this paper, I describe how in the aftermath of relational disruptions to giving of oneself and being received, shame inverts the passionate movement of responsibility toward and for others into an enclosed self-focused preoccupation. Through the filter of omnipotence and omniscience, shame transforms the helplessness of one’s suffering into punishment and fault. Self-shaming becomes paradoxically intertwined with self-pity, which then eventually leads to resentment and the entitlement of feeling owed, which is contrasted with the remorseful guilt that leads to a sense of owing others. Since a patient’s curiosity about the person of the therapist reflects an attempt to emerge from shame and take responsibility for what she comes to discover and know, it is of crucial importance that the therapist responds respectfully and actively engages that curiosity on a democratic rather than hierarchical basis.

    <artauth hidden="false"><aut listed="true" authindexid="Connolly, Medria" role="author" affid="aff0001" alias="false" asis="false"><nfirst type="ALLGIVEN" initials="M.">Medria</nfirst><nlast>Connolly</nlast><ndeg dgr="PHD"/><nbio>Medria Connolly, Ph.D., is a clinical psychologist in private practice in Santa Monica, California. She received her Ph.D. from Columbia University, and has been in practice for more than 30 years working with adolescents, adults and couples. In addition to her private practice, Dr. Connolly worked for many years as a consultant to a Los Angeles-based treatment program for adolescents in the juvenile justice system and in a high school-based health clinic in Watts. Her long-time work in these community settings, plus frequent contemporary encounters with implicit bias in professional groups, contributed to her recognition that individual, family and small group interventions are too limited in scope to alter the structural inequities confronting historically victimized groups, especially African-Americans. This recognition led to the embrace of a prospective national intervention, i.e., reparations, to address the underlying psychosocial challenges and promote racial healing. In addition, Dr. Connolly works as an organizational consultant to facilitate leadership, team building, communication and collaboration within diverse groups.</nbio></aut>
<aut listed="true" authindexid="Gobodo-Madikizela, Pumla" role="author" affid="aff0002" alias="false" asis="false"><nfirst type="ALLGIVEN" initials="P.">Pumla</nfirst><nlast>Gobodo-Madikizela</nlast><ndeg dgr="PHD"/><nbio>Pumla Gobodo-Madikizela, Ph.D., is Professor in the Faculty of Arts and Social Sciences at Stellenbosch University, where she holds the South African National Research Foundation Chair in Violent Histories and Transgenerational Trauma and the Research Chair in Historical Trauma and Transformation. She is the 2020-2021 Walter Jackson Bate Fellow at the Harvard Radcliffe Institute. Her research interest is in historical trauma and its intergenerational repercussions. She has published extensively on victims and perpetrators of gross human rights violations, on forgiveness, and remorse. Her books include the critically acclaimed A Human Being Died that Night: A Story of Forgiveness. She is editor and coauthor of several other academic publications.</nbio></aut>
<aut listed="true" authindexid="Layton, Lynne" role="author" affid="aff0003" alias="false" asis="false"><nfirst type="ALLGIVEN" initials="L.">Lynne</nfirst><nlast>Layton</nlast><ndeg dgr="PHD"/><nbio>
Lynne Layton, Ph.D., is a member of the Grassroots Reparations Campaign organizing committee. She is a psychoanalyst at the Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis and part-time faculty in the Department of Psychiatry at Harvard Medical School. She teaches Social Psychoanalysis in the Community, Liberation, Indigenous and Eco-Psychologies specialization at Pacifica Graduate Institute. She is Past-President of Section IX, Psychoanalysis for Social Responsibility, and the author of Toward a Social Psychoanalysis: Culture, Character, and Normative Unconscious Processes (Routledge, 2020, with Marianna Leavy-Sperounis).</nbio></aut>
<aut listed="true" authindexid="Nichols, Bryan" role="author" affid="aff0004" alias="false" asis="false"><nfirst type="ALLGIVEN" initials="B.">Bryan</nfirst><nlast>Nichols</nlast><ndeg dgr="PHD"/><nbio>Bryan Nichols, Ph.D., is a Los Angeles-based Clinical Psychologist with a practice focusing on teens, families, adults and couples. He received his Doctorate from UCLA, and was a long-time consultant and supervising psychologist for a Los Angeles city gang prevention and intervention program. Dr. Nichols’ work in both his private practice and the community has led to the recent development of societal, “macro-level” ideas about how to remediate persistent issues of bias that infect and undermine interracial relationships and the multi-disciplinary collaborations required to effectively implement community-based psycho-educational interventions. His early professional experiences included being a Trainer, and Trainer of Trainers in the “Effective Black Parenting Program,” and conducting numerous trainings in the “Dealing with Anger Program” designed for African-American youth.</nbio></aut>
<aut listed="true" authindexid="Pivnick, Billie" role="author" affid="aff0005" alias="false" asis="false"><nfirst type="ALLGIVEN" initials="B.">Billie</nfirst><nlast>Pivnick</nlast><ndeg dgr="PHD"/><nbio>Billie Pivnick, Ph.D., is a psychoanalytic psychologist who specializes in treating children and families confronting difficulties with traumatic loss, particularly those resulting from adoption and mass catastrophe. She is faculty/supervisor in the Columbia University Teachers College Doctoral Clinical Psychology Program and the William Alanson White Institute Child and Adolescent Psychotherapy Training Program, and is the former head of the Graduate Dance Therapy Program at Pratt Institute. She served as a consulting psychologist to Thinc Design, the exhibition designers partnered with the National September 11 Memorial Museum, Chicago’s Museum of Science and Industry, and the Smithsonian. Of her numerous publications, her essay “Spaces to stand in: Applying clinical psychoanalysis to the relational design of the National September 11 Memorial Museum” won the 2015 Schillinger Memorial Essay award.</nbio></aut>

Romy Reading   Romy Reading, Ph.D., is clinical psychologist in private practice in New York City, who received her Ph.D. from The New School for Social Research, and is currently a candidate at the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. She has worked as a Supervising Psychologist at the Center for Intensive Treatment of Personality Disorders at Mt. Sinai West and at the Peter Krueger Clinic for HIV/AIDS services at Mt. Sinai Beth Israel. She is also a co-host with Dr. Billie Pivnick of the podcast titled Couched, which features interdisciplinary conversations between psychoanalysts and experts from a wide array of fields. She has also coauthored manuscripts on the topics of therapist reflective functioning, mindfulness, and group psychotherapy for LGBT asylum seekers. She recently published a book review of Celia Brickman’s Race in Psychoanalysis: Aboriginal Populations in the Mind, in the International Journal of Psychoanalysis.CONTACT Romy Reading, Ph.D. romy@romyreading.com New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, 80 5th Avenue, 1406, New York, NY 10011

Couched is a podcast series that features conversations among psychoanalysts, artists, writers, activists, and intellectual change-makers about our current social and political conditions and the challenge of finding grounding and connection in an increasingly uncertain world. Couched aims to generate conversations that will move listeners to engage with one another and the world, ideally creating generative communities. This article is an edited transcription of one such conversation. It was recorded live at the 2021 Annual Spring Meeting of Division 39, The Society for Psychoanalysis and Psychoanalytic Psychology, of the American Psychological Association. In this episode our guests elucidate the challenges involved in providing reparations for the racial violence and genocide that characterized chattel slavery in the United States and apartheid in South Africa.

responseSet.title: Psychedelic Psychodynamics: Relational Knowing and the Unthought Known
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychedelic Psychodynamics: Relational Knowing and the Unthought Known

Kevin Barrett AM LCSW

Kevin Barrett

Chicago, Illinois, USA
barrettk@gmail.com

Kevin Barrett   Psychoanalytic psychotherapist in private practice in Chicago. He is a lecturer at the University of Chicago Crown Family School of Social Work, Policy, and Practice and a Clinical Associate Faculty Member at the Chicago Center for Psychoanalysis.

After a two-decade moratorium on psychedelic research in the United States, new studies on psychedelic assisted psychotherapy have shown promising results in the treatment of psychiatric disorders and the betterment of well people. Little has been written in contemporary psychoanalytic journals about the possible use of psychoanalytic theory as an underpinning for this research. This paper examines three concepts from psychoanalytic thinkers that may contribute to an understanding of what is curative in psychedelic assisted psychotherapy. First, I examine the possibility of psychedelics as a catalyst in changing a patient’s implicit relational patterns. Second, I use the work of Winnicott and Christopher Bollas to explore the psychedelic experience. Finally, I look at the role of mystical experience in psychedelic assisted psychotherapy.

Unpacking and Reworking Trauma

Neil Altman PHD

Neil Altman

Boston
altman.neil@gmail.com
60 Brattle St.#605, Cambridge, MA 02138, USA

Neil Altman   Psychoanalytic psychotherapist with many years of experience in community based community work, in public clinics and in private practice. He is currently cochair of the section on Community Psychoanalysis of the American Psychoanalytic Association. He is a member of the faculty at the William Alanson White Institute in New York City, and at the Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis in Boston, He is an Honorary Member of the William Alanson White Society, and Visiting faculty at Ambedkar University of Delhi, India. He is Editor Emeritus and Associate Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues, and on the editorial staff of The Journal of Infant, Child, and Adolescent Psychotherapy, The Journal of Child Psychotherapy, and The International Journal of Applied Psychoanalytic Studies. He is author of Psychoanalysis in Times of Accelerating Cultural Change: Spiritual Globalization (2015), The Analyst in the Inner City: Race, Class and Culture through a Psychoanalytic Lens, (2010), and White Privilege: Psychoanalytic Perspectives (2020). He is coauthor of Relational Child Psychotherapy (2002). Dr. Altman has published more than sixty articles in peer-reviewed journals.

In response to Dr. Tronick’s article on the evolution of responses to trauma, I explore the advantages of extending his study by differentiating between three types of trauma. I suggest that as analysts recognize chronic trauma that derives from pervasive racial and cultural prejudice, critical domains of social relevance open up for psychoanalytic consideration on levels of theory and practice. On a clinical level we are enabled to address suffering associated with social marginalization and neglect otherwise thought to be outside the realm of psychoanalytic applicability.
